naturalMeaning: Legally in Japan, water must meet a mineral-content or temperature threshold at the source to be called 温泉 — not every hot bath qualifies.

examples.text: この辺りは温泉で有名です。
examples.reading: このあたりはおんせんでゆうめいです。
examples.romaji: Kono atari wa onsen de yūmei desu.
examples.translation: This area is famous for its hot springs.

naturalMeaning: Distinguished from a hotel (ホテル) mainly by tatami rooms, futon bedding, an included multi-course dinner, and staff who look after your room.

examples.text: 今晩、予約をしています。
examples.reading: こんばん、よやくをしています。
examples.translation: I have a reservation for tonight.

reading: いっぱくにしょくつき
translation: one night with two meals included
naturalMeaning: The standard ryokan rate plan: one night's stay plus dinner and breakfast. It's how most ryokan list prices, so it's worth recognizing on a booking page.
The cheaper alternative to 一泊二食付き — you skip the included dinner and breakfast, common if you'd rather eat out.
Traditionally the public face of the inn — greeting guests, checking on the dinner, sometimes even the owner's spouse rather than an employee.
Brings your tea on arrival, serves your in-room dinner, and lays out your futon in the evening — often the same person for your whole stay.
Open to the air, often overlooking a garden, mountain, or the sea — usually the highlight photo of any ryokan's listing.

examples.text: 露天風呂からの景色がきれいです。
examples.reading: ろてんぶろからのけしきがきれいです。
examples.romaji: Rotenburo kara no keshiki ga kirei desu.
examples.translation: The view from the outdoor bath is beautiful.

naturalMeaning: Booked in advance for a set time slot (often 45 minutes), so couples, families, or the tattooed and the anxious can bathe without an audience.
Increasingly rare — most modern onsen split baths by gender (男湯/女湯), sometimes alternating which side is which by time of day.

translation: rinsing off with hot water before entering the bath
naturalMeaning: A quick scoop or two of hot water over the body at the edge of the tub, done after washing and right before stepping in — not optional.
The row of low stools and shower heads where everyone washes and rinses thoroughly before ever approaching the tub itself.
Used to wash and for modesty walking to the tub, but it never goes in the water itself — most bathers fold it and rest it on their head or set it aside at the tub's edge.
The word you'll see on signage as 入れ墨・タトゥーのある方はご遠慮ください ("those with tattoos are asked to refrain") — see the culture notes for why, and for workarounds.
A skin-colored adhesive patch sold or lent at some onsen specifically to cover a small tattoo so it stays out of sight.

translation: feeling faint or unwell from soaking too long

naturalMeaning: A soft, custardy egg slow-cooked at onsen bathing temperature rather than boiled — a common souvenir snack at onsen towns.

translation: fresh, non-recirculated spring water (constantly flowing in and out)
naturalMeaning: A point of pride on ryokan signage — the water runs straight from the source and out again rather than being filtered and reused, considered the mark of a serious onsen.

translation: hot-spring hopping (visiting several baths in one trip)
naturalMeaning: Many onsen towns sell a 湯めぐり pass — a set of tickets letting you try several different inns' baths without staying the night at each.
A longer, old-fashioned style of onsen visit — historically several days to weeks at one spring for a specific ailment, rather than a single overnight trip.
Marketing shorthand for a remote, hard-to-reach onsen — often mountain inns reachable only by a long drive or a hike.
A town built around a cluster of onsen, usually with a strollable main street of foot baths, souvenir shops, and guests wandering in yukata after dinner.
Provided in your ryokan room to wear around the inn, to dinner, and even out into the onsen town — not just sleepwear.

translation: wrapped with the right side closest to the body (the correct way to wear a yukata)
naturalMeaning: Confusingly named — it means the right side goes on first, next to your skin, so the left panel ends up on top. Wrapping it the other way (左前) is reserved for dressing the dead, so it's worth getting right.

The elaborate small-plate dinner typical of a ryokan stay — seasonal, often ten or more courses. Not to be confused with 懐石料理 (chakaiseki), the sparser meal that precedes a tea ceremony; they're pronounced identically but written differently.
The small printed card listing each course of the kaiseki dinner by name, often left at your place setting as a keepsake.
Sake brewed regionally rather than a big national brand — ryokan dinners often pair the kaiseki courses with one from the local area.

translation: Japanese bedding (mattress and quilt laid on tatami)

naturalMeaning: At most ryokan, the 仲居 does this for you while you're at dinner — the room you left as a sitting area is a bedroom by the time you return.

lemma: チェックインをお願いします
translation: I'd like to check in, please.

lemma: 何時からお風呂に入れますか
reading: なんじからおふろにはいれますか
romaji: Nanji kara ofuro ni hairemasu ka.
translation: From what time can I use the bath?

lemma: 入れ墨があっても大丈夫ですか
reading: いれずみがあってもだいじょうぶですか
romaji: Irezumi ga atte mo daijōbu desu ka.
translation: Is it okay even if I have a tattoo?

lemma: 貸切風呂を予約したいです
reading: かしきりぶろをよやくしたいです
translation: I'd like to reserve a private bath.

lemma: 浴衣のサイズを変えてもらえますか
reading: ゆかたのさいずをかえてもらえますか
romaji: Yukata no saizu o kaete moraemasu ka.
translation: Could I get a different yukata size?

lemma: タオルは部屋にありますか
reading: タオルはへやにありますか
romaji: Taoru wa heya ni arimasu ka.
translation: Is there a towel in the room?

lemma: チェックアウトは何時ですか
reading: チェックアウトはなんじですか
romaji: Chekku auto wa nanji desu ka.
